headlineSummary: HN sentiment was mixed overall: Anthropic drew the most positive discussion, Gemini was mostly mixed-to-neutral, and OpenAI and Microsoft Copilot were both negative with a close tie and limited evidence separating them [E2][E5][E6].

ranking.summary: Microsoft Copilot was negative on limited evidence, mainly because one commenter said it does little beyond opening a chat side panel and called Copilot for Excel useless [E2].
OpenAI leaned negative, with praise for ChatGPT for Excel offset by criticism of spreadsheet performance, privacy concerns, and a security-bypass complaint [E1][E3][E4][E6].

Google Gemini drew mixed reactions: one commenter found its Sheets integration very poor, another reported a mostly successful rover test, and a third raised privilege concerns around Gemini in Docs [E5][E8][E9].
Anthropic was viewed positively overall, with praise for Claude’s content and design quality, though one comment raised privacy and HIPAA concerns [E2][E5][E10].

annotations.rationale: The title explicitly names ChatGPT and reads as a product release announcement without clear praise or criticism.

evidence.excerpt: This looks bad for Microsoft. They added a Copilot button to all their products but it doesn't do much more than open a chat side panel. I recently tried Claude Cowork for PowerPoint and I was stunned by the content as well as design quality of the deck it produced. That's a threat for Microsoft because now you don't need the editing tools of PowerPoint, AI replaces it, so all you need is the presentation mode of PowerPoint. Copilot for Excel is useless. Ask it what is in cell A1 and it can't answer. I am looking forward to trying ChatGPT for Excel.

annotations.rationale: The comment says Copilot 'doesn't do much more than open a chat side panel' and later that 'Copilot for Excel is useless.'
Claude Cowork is praised with 'I was stunned by the content as well as design quality of the deck it produced.'
The speaker says 'I am looking forward to trying ChatGPT for Excel,' indicating mild favorable expectation.

evidence.excerpt: I've experimented with ChatGPT for spreadsheets the past 6 months, and while the results look nice now it has been excruciatingly slow for even the simplest spreadsheet. I'm talking 15-20 minutes to make some pretty basic calculator with graphs. IIRC, it used a lot of time purely on the styling.

annotations.rationale: The comment criticizes ChatGPT for spreadsheet work as 'excruciatingly slow' even on simple tasks, though it notes the results look nice.

evidence.excerpt: However, in this case I would be somewhat cautious, especially as "your chats, attachments, and workbook content — may be shared with OpenAI" (as per the Microsoft Marketplace description: https://marketplace.microsoft.com/en-us/product/WA200010215?...). This seems like a security nightmare, which is especially relevant because sensitive data is often stored in Excel files.

annotations.rationale: The commenter highlights that workbook content 'may be shared with OpenAI' and calls that 'a security nightmare'.

evidence.excerpt: I’ve always found it unbelievable how bad Gemini’s Google Sheets interaction is. Copying the sheets into Claude and then modifying them there and copying them back actually outperforms it. Nowadays I just make single-purpose websites with Claude Code because Google Sheets has such poor AI integration and is outrageously tedious to edit. They had all the parts and I have a subscription and it still does terrible things like prompt me to use pandas after exporting as a CSV. It will mention some cell and then can’t read it. It can’t edit tables so they just get overwritten with other tables it generates.

annotations.rationale: The comment says Gemini's Sheets interaction is 'unbelievable how bad' and lists multiple failures like not reading cells and overwriting tables.
Claude and Claude Code are presented as better alternatives, with Claude explicitly said to 'actually' outperform the Gemini/Sheets workflow.

evidence.excerpt: Last week someone posted an article here about ChatGPT using Cloudflare Turnstile. [0] First, the article made some mistakes how it works. Second, I used the [AI company product] and the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) to completely rewrite all the scripts intercepting them before they were evaluated -- the same way I was able to figure out PerimeterX in 3 days -- and then recursively solve controlling all the finger printing so that it controls the profile. Then it created an API proxy to expose ChatGPT for free. It required some coaching about the technique but it did most of the work in 3 hours. These companies are spending 10s of millions of dollars on these products and considering what OpenAI is boasting about security, they are worthless.

annotations.rationale: The commenter claims ChatGPT protections were bypassed quickly and concludes that, despite OpenAI's security boasts, 'they are worthless.'
The title is a straightforward Gemini-branded release/update mention without evaluative wording.

evidence.excerpt: As the article notes regular Gemini and Gemma also have spatial reasoning capabilities, which I decided to test by seeing if Gemini could drive a little rover successfully, which it mostly did:

annotations.rationale: The commenter reports a successful personal test, saying Gemini could drive a rover 'successfully, which it mostly did.'

evidence.excerpt: If I use a document processor (like Google Docs) to compose a message to my attorney, which message itself would be privileged, but I use some sidebar feature of Google Docs/Gemini to clean up a sentence that I thought was clunky, and elsewhere I have, for whatever reason, enabled features that permit Google to use inputs and outputs to train or refine their models, has that destroyed the privilege?

annotations.rationale: The commenter raises a concern that using Google Docs/Gemini, especially with training-enabled features, may destroy attorney-client privilege.

evidence.excerpt: Love it! Do you have a BAA with Claude though? Otherwise, your demo is likely exposing PHI to 3rd parties and exposing you to risk related to HIPAA

annotations.rationale: The commenter questions Claude's HIPAA/BAA suitability and warns of PHI exposure to third parties, expressing privacy/compliance concern.
